title: Bhartrihari's Vakyapadiya — Sphota and the Unity of Meaning
subtitle: The Sphota theory — meaning as holistic revelation; the three levels of speech (vaikharī/madhyamā/paśyantī) as the source of Trika doctrine; śabda-brahman as the Logos

summary.one_line: Bhartrihari's Vākyapadīya analyzed as the foundational text of linguistic metaphysics: sphota as the revelation of meaning, the three levels of speech as the model for Trika's vāc-triad, and śabda-...
summary.scope: Covers the Brahma-kāṇḍa (Kāṇḍa 1) on śabda-brahman and sphota theory, the Vākya-kāṇḍa (Kāṇḍa 2) on the sentence and its meaning, the three levels of speech, the doctrine of indivisible sentence-meaning, and the relationship of grammar to liberation.

body.text: [See VP I.1: 'Anādi-nidhanaṁ brahma śabda-tattvaṁ yad akṣaram / vivartate 'rtha-bhāvena prakriyā jagato yataḥ' — 'Brahman, without beginning or end, the imperishable Word-essence, transforms into objects — from which the world-process proceeds.' Bhartrihari's śabda-brahman is the Logos: the universe is not merely described by language but is language. Compare to John 1.1 ('In the beginning was the Word') and the Tantraloka's doctrine of parā-vāc as the supreme speech that is both consciousness and its expression. Śabda-brahman is the daimon as Logos — the principle that the world is meaning before it is matter.]

[See VP I.44-48. Sphota (from sphuṭ — 'to burst forth') is that from which meaning bursts forth. The sphota is the unitary linguistic unit — whether phoneme, word, or sentence — that is revealed through the uttered sounds but is distinct from them. The sounds (dhvani) manifest the sphota, but the sphota is not composed of them; it is a whole that is grasped in a single flash of recognition (pratibhā). This is the unus-ambo principle applied to language: the sphota is both one (the indivisible meaning-unit) and two (it appears through sequential sounds but is grasped instantaneously). Compare to the daimon as the 'one and the two' — the principle that mediates between the temporal and the eternal.]

[See VP I.142-144. Bhartrihari describes three levels of speech: paśyantī (the seeing speech — undifferentiated, intuitive), madhyamā (the middle speech — the mental formulation), and vaikharī (the articulated speech — audible utterance). 'The seers perceive speech in three stages: paśyantī, madhyamā, and vaikharī.' This is the source of the Trika doctrine of three levels of vāc (parā, paśyantī, madhyamā, vaikharī — Abhinavagupta expands to four). Paśyantī is the level where speech and consciousness are identical — the daimon's own language. Madhyamā is the level of interior dialogue, the language of the soul conversing with itself. Vaikharī is the world of articulate sound, the body of the daimon.]

[See VP I.14-22. Grammar (vyākaraṇa) is the door to liberation: 'The grammarian's discipline is the first step of the Vedas (chandasām prathamaṅgam); it is the most sacred, the light of the celestial path; it is the door to freedom (apavargasya dvāram); it is the supreme abode of all sciences; it is the royal road for those seeking liberation.' Grammar purifies speech (vāk-śuddhi) by removing impurities from language, and since language is the fabric of reality, purifying language is purifying consciousness. This is the paṇḍita's path to the daimon: by mastering the structure of the Logos, one becomes the Logos. Compare to the Abramelin method of mastering the divine names.]

[See VP II.1-86. The central thesis of Kāṇḍa 2: the sentence is not a composite of word-meanings but an indivisible whole (akhaṇḍa-pakṣa). 'The meaning of a sentence is a single, indivisible insight (pratibhā) that is grasped all at once.' This is the unus-ambo in language: the sentence is one (a single meaning-unit) and many (composed of words that seem to have independent meaning). The word-meanings are abstractions from the sentence-whole — they have no independent reality. This is identical to the Trika doctrine of the universe as a single sentence spoken by Śiva, where each tattva is a word that only has meaning within the whole. The pratibhā that grasps sentence-meaning is the same flash of intuition by which the soul knows the daimon.]

[See VP I.123-130 and II.119-146. Pratibhā is the flash of intuitive insight that grasps meaning — it is not produced by the sequence of sounds but is revealed through them. Bhartrihari describes it as the innate intelligence (sahaja-pratibhā) that is the ground of all linguistic understanding. This pratibhā is the daimon's mode of communication: the sudden 'bursting forth' (sphoṭa) of meaning that is not built up from parts but given whole. In the Trika system, pratibhā is the icchā-śakti (the will-power) of Śiva — the spontaneous self-revelation of consciousness. The daimon contact is a pratibhā-event: the meaning of one's existence is grasped in a flash, not assembled from experiences.]

[See VP I.1-5 and the discussion of time (kāla) in the Brahma-kāṇḍa. Bhartrihari identifies time with the śabda-brahman: time is the power of the Word that unfolds the universe sequentially. 'Time, which is one, appears as many due to the differences in the actions it governs; its powers are inseparable from the Word-principle.' This makes time a function of speech — the sequential unfolding of the sphoṭa in the form of sentences, words, and phonemes. The daimon's time is the time of the Word — not chronological but kairotic: the moment of pratibhā when meaning bursts forth. Compare to Corbin's 'temporality of the imaginal' — the time of the daimon is the time of revelation.]

[See VP I.1 and commentary. Bhartrihari uses the term vivarta (appearance, manifestation) for the relationship between śabda-brahman and the world — the world is not a real transformation (pariṇāma) of the Word but an apparent manifestation, like light appearing as different colors through a prism. This vivarta-vāda is the same doctrine found in Advaita Vedānta and in the Trika doctrine of ābhāsa (manifestation as reflection). The world is the Light of Consciousness (prakāśa) appearing as the multiplicity of objects through the power of reflective awareness (vimarśa). This is the daimon's own nature: one light appearing as many souls.]

issues.description: Currently referencing source synthesis. Real passages need to be extracted from the Sanskrit text and translations.

issues.title: Missing treatment of Bhartrihari's influence on Kashmiri Shaivism
issues.description: Abhinavagupta and Kṣemarāja's extensive use of Bhartrihari's three-levels of speech and sphoṭa theory needs detailed mapping.

versions.summary: Initial RO created. 8 passages covering śabda-brahman, sphoṭa theory, three levels of speech, grammar as liberation, sentence-meaning indivisibility, pratibhā, time-as-speech, and vivarta-vāda.
